title: Phase-slip residual-order spin state in FeSe
authors: Zhixin Liu, Jiyu Fan, Lei Zhang, Ma Chunlan, Yanda Ji, Zhongqin Yang, Yan Zhu
abstract: Clarifying the magnetic ground state is essential for analysing unconventional superconductivity, because microscopic magnetic order provides one of the basic starting assumptions for spin-fluctuation pairing theories. FeSe exhibits pronounced stripe- and Neel-type spin fluctuations yet lacks static long-range order, posing a long-standing puzzle. By combining PBE and r2SCAN mixed exchange-correlation calculations with spectrally weighted simulations of the static spin structure factor S(q), we show that FeSe is not governed by a single magnetic configuration but by a nearly degenerate manifold of phase-slip defects embedded in a stripe background. We term this state a residual-order spin state (ROSS): a spin state that retains local stripe-like antiferromagnetic correlations but loses long-range phase coherence because of phase slips. Multiple slip configurations are compressed into an exceptionally narrow energy window. Non-local magneto-elastic coupling redistributes domain-wall formation energy through the lattice, whereas competing magnetic interactions truncate the real-space coherence length to an optimal scale of about ten moments. Weighted superpositions of these slip states reproduce the momentum-space line shapes of both stripe- and Neel-type spin fluctuations observed by inelastic neutron scattering, providing a microscopic basis for superconductivity models built on spin fluctuations.
summary: This paper proposes that the lack of long-range magnetic order in FeSe arises from a 'residual-order spin state' composed of a manifold of nearly degenerate phase-slip defects. By modeling the system as a superposition of these slip states, the authors quantitatively replicate the coexistence of stripe- and Neel-type spin fluctuations seen in neutron scattering. This provides a microscopic basis for understanding how local magnetic correlations drive superconductivity in iron-based materials.
main_problem: Resolving the long-standing puzzle of the magnetic ground state in FeSe, which exhibits strong stripe- and Neel-type spin fluctuations but lacks static long-range order.
main_result: The authors propose a 'Residual-Order Spin State' (ROSS) where a nearly degenerate manifold of phase-slip defects embedded in a stripe background destroys long-range coherence while preserving local correlations, successfully reproducing experimental neutron scattering spectra.
method: Density Functional Theory (DFT) using a mixed PBE and r2SCAN exchange-correlation functional, combined with spectrally weighted simulations of the static spin structure factor S(q) through a weighted superposition of slip configurations.
model_or_system: The physical system is FeSe (Iron Selenide), a non-doped superconductor. The proposed model is the ROSS, characterized by a stripe-antiferromagnetic background containing low-energy pi-phase slip domain walls.
key_observables: Static spin structure factor S(q), momentum-space line shapes (linewidths and peak positions) in the stripe and Neel channels, and magneto-elastic coupling forces.
important_parameters: Correlation length N (number of magnetic moments), exchange-correlation mixing ratio (45% PBE / 55% r2SCAN), and energy-dependent renormalization of N (from 10 to 8 at high energies).
main_assumptions: The calculation computes an effective S(q) within a specific energy window rather than the full dynamical structure factor S(q, omega); experimental data were digitized from published figures.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of ROSS and phase-slip mechanism; Fig 2: Energetic landscape of competing magnetic states and XC-functional dependence; Fig 3: Magneto-elastic response/force fields; Fig 4: Comparison of calculated S(q) fits to experimental INS datasets across different energy regimes.
paper_structure: Introduction of the FeSe magnetic puzzle; presentation of the ROSS model and DFT methodology; analysis of the energetic degeneracy of magnetic configurations; investigation of magneto-elastic coupling; reconstruction of S(q) via weighted superposition; comparison with experimental neutron scattering data; conclusion.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic overview of the phase-slip residual-order spin state (ROSS). A 𝜋phase slip in a stripe-antiferromagnetic background truncates long-range phase coherence while preserving local stripe-like correlations. The spin reversal across the slip wall generates short-range Néel-like anticorrelations, so that 𝑆(q) = | Í 𝑗𝑚𝑗𝑒−𝑖q·r 𝑗|2 contains both stripe- and Néel-channel fingerprints. Schematic line cuts illustrate the resulting spectral-weight redistribution; quantitative INS comparisons are shown in Fig. 4.

Figure 2: Frustrated near-degenerate magnetic manifold in FeSe tuned by exchange-correlation mixing. (a) Energy differences Δ𝐸= 𝐸−𝐸SAFM (meV/Fe) of the TRI and PCB states relative to SAFM as a function of the PBE mixing ratio in the mixed PBE–𝑟2SCAN scheme. The dashed line marks degeneracy with SAFM, and the inset enlarges the near-degenerate region. (b) Relative energies of competing magnetic states under the representative mixing condition selected from (a). The broken y-axis separates the low-energy quasi-degenerate manifold from the higher-energy states.

Figure 3: Non-local magneto-elastic response and slip-length energetics.(a) Force field induced by a representative 3\7 phase-slip wall on the relaxed SAFM lattice. Black arrows denote total atomic forces, and red/blue arrows denote Fe spins. (b) Effective slip-wall formation energy as a function of slip length, showing a minimum near 3\7 (𝑁= 𝑎+ 𝑏≈10) and an energy barrier near 3\9.

Figure 4: Weighted slip-state reconstruction of the INS line shapes. (a) Schematic of the weighted superposition procedure and comparison with three low-energy INS datasets. Structure factors from slip configurations with fixed correlation length 𝑁= 𝐿+ 𝑅= 10 are combined as 𝑆fit(𝑞) = Í 𝐿|𝑅𝛼𝐿|𝑅𝑆𝐿|𝑅(𝑞), with Í 𝐿|𝑅𝛼𝐿|𝑅= 1. The right panel compares the weighted spectrum with Experiment 1 at 𝑇= 5 K and 𝐸= 4 ± 1 meV, Experiment 2 at 𝑇= 1.5 K and 𝐸= 4 meV, and Experiment 3 at 𝑇= 2 K and 𝐸= 5.625 ± 0.125 meV. (b) Fit to Experiment 1 at 𝑇= 5 K and 𝐸= 10 ± 2 meV. (c) Fit to Experiment 1 at 𝑇= 5 K and 𝐸= 20 ± 5 meV. (d) Fit to Experiment 1 at 𝑇= 5 K and 𝐸= 35 ± 5 meV, comparing the results obtained...
